sources.notes: Hard version permits any interval 0<=l<=r<2^30 and asks for an optimal reordered copy.
Official solution recursively splits at the highest differing bit and pairs values across the split; it also describes an alternative reversed-bit binary trie greedy.

annotation.summary: For an arbitrary integer interval [l,r], permute one copy to maximize the sum of bitwise OR with the fixed ordered copy.
annotation.constraints: The total interval length is up to 2e5, but values are up to 2^30, so the construction must recurse over bits rather than over value universe.
annotation.core_idea: At each highest bit where the current interval contains both 0 and 1, pair as many numbers as possible across that split so the high bit contributes to every paired OR. The optimal partner around the split t is 2t-1-x, which mirrors lower bits and keeps the paired values as bit-disjoint as possible. Any unpaired prefix or suffix becomes the same problem on lower bits.

solution_variants.name: highest-bit-divide-and-conquer
solution_variants.summary: Recursively split the interval by the current bit and mirror-pair values across the first 0/1 boundary.

solution_variants.name: reversed-bit-trie-greedy
solution_variants.summary: Insert reversed-bit values into a binary trie and greedily choose the opposite bit when possible for each fixed index.

tags.evidence: Optimality is reasoned bit by bit, especially at the highest differing bit of the interval.
After cross-pairing at the current bit split, the remaining prefix and suffix are solved recursively on lower bits.
The algorithm must output the actual reordered array, not only the maximum sum.
The alternative trie construction greedily picks the opposite bit whenever a remaining number exists.
